locked_facts: Average power: P_avg = W/t (total work divided by total time)
Instantaneous power: P = F·v = Fv cos(θ)
Power is the rate of doing work — a scalar quantity with SI unit watt (W)
For a car on level road at constant velocity: P = fv, where f is friction/drag
truth_statements: Power is defined as the rate at which work is done or energy is transferred
Instantaneous power equals the dot product of force and velocity vectors at that instant
Efficiency of a machine is always less than 100% due to energy losses (friction, heat, sound)
A more powerful engine does the same work in less time, or more work in the same time
The area under a power-time graph gives the total work done (energy transferred)

aha_moment.what_to_show: Two identical cars climbing the same hill — one in 10 seconds, the other in 20 seconds. Both do the same work (mgh), but the faster car has DOUBLE the power. P-t graphs side by side: same area (same work), different heights (different power).
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Work bars equal. Power bars 2:1 ratio. P-t graph areas identical. Time bars 1:2 ratio.
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that power is NOT the same as work — same work at different rates means different power

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: A block being pulled by force F at velocity v along a surface. P = Fv readout visible. Work accumulator W and time counter t shown. P_avg = W/t displayed.
simulation_states.emphasis: Introduce power as rate of work — not the same as total work done

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Block moves. Work accumulates over time. P-t graph plots instantaneous power. Area under P-t curve grows — this area IS the total work done. Two scenarios compared: same force, different velocities.
simulation_states.emphasis: P = Fv means higher speed at same force = more power consumed

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: Two cars climbing same hill. Car A: fast, high power, short time. Car B: slow, low power, long time. Both do work = mgh. P-t graphs show same area but different shapes. Efficiency comparison: η = useful work / total input.
simulation_states.emphasis: Same work done but different power — power is about RATE, not amount

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust force, velocity, and efficiency. See instantaneous power P = Fv update. Add friction to see input power vs output power. Calculate efficiency. Convert between W, HP, kWh.
simulation_states.emphasis: Vary velocity to see power change. Add losses to see efficiency drop. 1 HP = 746 W.

common_misconceptions.belief: More power means more work is done
common_misconceptions.correction: Power is the RATE of doing work, not the amount. A 100W motor and a 50W motor can do the same work — the 100W motor just does it in half the time. P = W/t, so for same W, higher P means less t.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Two cars doing same work mgh — one with double power finishes in half time. Work bars equal, power bars different.

common_misconceptions.belief: Efficiency can be 100% in real machines
common_misconceptions.correction: No real machine achieves 100% efficiency. Friction, air resistance, heat, and sound always dissipate some energy. η = P_output/P_input is always < 100% for real machines.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show energy flow diagram: input → useful output + losses. Losses are always nonzero.

legend.meaning: Total work done over time interval
